narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical student's average of 3.21. However, the admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted an 88.8% chance that you would not pass it on your first try—a threshold that led to the regrettable rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on that critical test. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school lagged significantly behind most peers. Close behind was your LSAT score of 25, far under the dataset's average of 36.7, which strongly reinforced the model's concern about bar exam readiness compared to typical successful students. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it exceeds what the model sees in most applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 1 had a milder negative pull, while your middle family income bracket nudged slightly toward failure relative to averages, and your full-time student status offered a small positive boost, as it's the norm for 93% of students. Overall, these elements combined to paint a picture riskier than the profiles of students who typically pass the bar on their first attempt. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model weighs these metrics heavily for fairness and outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above 36.7, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or context in a new application, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and extracurriculars. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the odds dramatically in your favor. We're rooting for you—law needs voices like yours. Keep pushing.
